title: In re Estates of Moylan and Moylan - Decision and Order
text:
r~· :_ _ ;~::n
SUf:i[ lUS~ COUI1T
n G~_l.4! .. l
2026 APR -3 PM ~: 51
2 CLEF!\ OF COURT
3
4
IN THE SUPERIOR COURT OF GUAM
5
6
IN THE MATTER OF THE ESTATES PROBATE CASE NO. PR0234-22
7
OF
8
DECISION AND ORDER
9 FRANCIS L. MOYLAN and YUK LAN
MOYLAN,
10
Decedents.
II
12 INTRODUCTION
13
Two testamentary instruments are at issue: a handwritten will executed by Francis L.
14
Moylan in 1993 and a formal will executed by his wife, Yuk Lan Moylan, in 2008. Objector
15
asks the Court to set both aside-invoking lack of capacity, undue influence, and alleged
16
defects in execution-largely by pointing to family discord and decades-old guardianship
17
18 proceedings. But probate litigation is not sustained by suspicion or hindsight disagreement with
19
a testator's decisions. Guam law presumes the validity of duly executed wills, enforces
20
holographic wills that satisfy statutory requirements, and demands clear, competent evidence
21
before a court may disregard testamentary intent. That evidence is absent here. The undisputed
22
23 record establishes that Francis Moylan's holographic will satisfies 15 GCA § 207, that Yuk Lan
24
Moylan's will was properly executed, and that claims of undue influence and incapacity rest on
25
conjecture rather than proof. Because no genuine dispute of material fact exists, summary
26
judgment is required. Petitioner Lina Leialoha Moylan Alston is represented by Attorney
27
Jacqueline T. Terlaje and Objector Jeffrey S. Moylan is represented by Attorney William B.
28
Pole.

Decision and Order
PR0234-22
In The Matter of The Estates Of Francis L. Moylan and Yuk Lan Moylan
BACKGROUND
2
This probate matter concerns the estates of Francis L. Moylan and Yuk Lan Moylan,
3
husband and wife. Francis L. Moylan-also known as "Scotty" Moylan-died on August 24,
4
2010. Yuk Lan Moylan died on November 20, 2021. Following their deaths, competing filings
5
6 were made concerning the administration of both estates and the validity of their respective
7 testamentary instruments.
8
On December 21, 2022, Petitioner Lina Leialoha Moylan Alston ("Petitioner"), th
9
decedents' daughter, filed a Verified Petition for Probate of Will with Will Annexed an
10
Issuance of Letters Testamentary for the Estate of Yuk Lan Moylan and sought letters o
11
12 administration for the Estate of Francis L. Moylan based on his holographic will dated Janua
13
31, 1993. The Verified Petition alleged that Francis Moylan intentionally omitted certai
14
children from his holographic will in favor of his surviving spouse, Yuk Lan Moylan, and tha
15
Yuk Lan Moylan later executed a formal Last Will and Testament on March 19, 2008, namin
16
Petitioner as her testamentary appointee.
17
18 Richard E. Moylan (Objector), an interested party, filed an Objection on December 23,
19
2022, followed by an Objection and Will Contest on February 1, 2023, and a Verified Statemen
20
and Will Contest on June 14, 2023. Richard contested both the holographic will of Francis
21
Moylan and the 2008 will of Yuk Lan Moylan, asserting claims of lack of capacity, undu
22
23 influence, and improper execution.
24 On January 3, 2023, the Court granted the parties time to conduct discovery and ordere
25
that all dispositive motions be filed by March 13, 2024. A hearing on the Verified Petition was
26
held on July 20, 2023. On July 24, 2023, the Court entered an Order After Hearing, admitting the
27
28
Page 2 oflO

Decision and Order
PR0234-22
In The Matter of The Estates Of Francis L. Moylan and Yuk Lan Moylan
holographic will to probate and finding that the statutory requirements of 15 GCA § 207 ha
been satisfied.
2
3 The dispute over Yuk Lan Moylan's capacity arises against the backdrop of extensive
4
guardianship litigation spanning more than a decade. In March 2008-the same period in whic
5
Yuk Lan Moylan executed her will-the guardianship court initially entered a finding o
6
incompetence. That finding was later reversed. On appeal, the Supreme Court of Guam affirme
7
the guardianship court's determination that Yuk Lan Moylan possessed the ability to exercise
8
9 adequate judgment and that, so long as her preferences were reasonable, respecting those
10
,r,r
preferences was important to her continued well-being. In re Moylan, 2011 Guam 16, 8, 22
11
(Oct. 18, 2011).
12
On March 13, 2024, Petitioner filed the present Motion for Summary Judgment, seekin
13
14 dismissal of Richard's will contest and admission of both testamentary instruments to probate.
15 The motion asserts that no genuine dispute of material fact exists as to due execution,
16
testamentary capacity, undue influence, or the validity of Francis Moylan' s holographic will, an
17
that the competency issue relating to Yuk Lan Moylan is foreclosed by prior guardianship rulings
18
affirmed on appeal.
19
20 The Objector opposed, asserting that genuine disputes remain regarding testamentar
21
capacity, authenticity, undue influence, and the effect of the prior guardianship proceedings. Th
22
court subsequently entered an order finding good cause that Jeffrey S. Moylan (Objector) shall
23
be substituted as Plaintiff in this matter in place of Richard E. Moylan. The summary judgmen
24
motion is now fully briefed and before the court for resolution.
25
26
27
28
Page 3 oflO

Decision and Order
PR0234-22
In The Matter of The Estates Of Francis L. Moylan and Yuk Lan Moylan
DISCUSSION
The Court begins by explaining why both wills are entitled to a presumption of vali
2
3 execution under Guam law and why Objector has failed to rebut that presumption wit
4
competent evidence. It then addresses the validity of Francis L. Moylan's holographic will unde
5
15 GCA § 207 and concludes that the undisputed record satisfies the statute as a matter of law.
6
Next, the Court considers Objector's undue-influence claims and explains why they fall short o
7
the strict statutory standard governing such challenges. The Court then turns to Objector's
8
9 competency argument, explaining why the prior guardianship proceedings-rather than
undermining Yuk Lan Moylan's will-foreclose the assertion that she lacked testamentar
II
capacity when she executed it. Finally, applying Guam's summary-judgment standard, the Cou
12
concludes that no genuine dispute of material fact exists and that Petitioners are entitled to
13
14 judgment as a matter oflaw.
15
I. · The Wills Are Presumed Validly Executed Under Guam Law
16
17 Guam law favors the validity of testamentary instruments and recognizes a presumptio
18
of due execution once a will appears on its face to comply with statutory requirements. In r
19
Estate of Hemlani, 2008 Guam 25 ,r 10-11 (May 5, 2007). Proof of the genuineness of th
20
testator's signature-and, where applicable, compliance with statutory execution requirements
21
establishes a prima facie case that the will was validly executed, shifting the burden to the
22
23 contestant to produce clear and convincing evidence of invalidity. Id. In Hemlani, the Gua
24
Supreme Court affirmed admission of a contested will where the probate court relied on th
25
totality of the circumstances, rejecting arguments that technical deficiencies or conflictin
26
testimony defeated execution. Id. at 55. Once due execution is shown, speculative attacks,
· 27
28
Page 4 of 10

Decision and Order
PR0234-22
In The Matter of The Estates Of Francis L. Moylan and Yuk Lan Moylan
disagreement with the dispositive scheme, or conjecture regarding the testator's intent
insufficient to overcome the presumption. Id.
2
3 Those principles apply with here-to both the will of Francis L. Moylan and the will o
4
Yuk Lan Moylan. As to Francis L. Moylan, the record establishes that the proffered will bears
5
his handwritten signature and reflects testamentary intent on its face. Nothing in the recor
6
suggests forgery, mechanical authorship, or third-party alteration. The same presumptio
7
attaches independently to Yuk Lan Moylan's will. The Objector's challenge to Yuk La
8
9 Moylan's will does not rest on evidence that statutory execution requirements were unmet, bu
10
instead on generalized assertions questioning the circumstances surrounding the will and its
II
ultimate dispositions. Guam law does not permit such conjecture to defeat a will. Once a will is
12
facially valid and signed by the testator, it is presumed duly executed. Where, as here, both will
13
14 independently satisfy Guam's execution requirements and are supported by the presumption o
15 due execution, the burden rests squarely on the Objector to come forward with evidenc
16
sufficient to create a triable issue of fact. The Objector has failed to meet that burden as to eithe
17
will. Unsupported allegations, hindsight disagreement with testamentary choices, and speculative
18
theories do not constitute evidence capable of rebutting the presumption recognized by Gua
19
20 law.
21
Because no genuine dispute of material fact exists regarding due execution of either will,
22
summary judgment is appropriate.
23
24
25
26
27
28
Page 5 oflO

Decision and Order
PR0234-22
In The Matter of The Estates Of Francis L. Moylan and Yuk Lan Moylan
II. The Holographic Will Satisfies the Requirements of 15 GCA § 207 and Is Valid
as a Matter of Law
2
Guam law expressly recognizes holographic wills. 15 GCA § 207 provides that a will is
3
valid if the signature and written, dated and signed by the hand of the testator himself. The
4
statute's Commentary confirms that this provision was intended to relax formal execution
5
6 requirements and to uphold testamentary intent where authenticity is not in doubt, even in the
7
absence of witnesses, attestation clauses, or strict compliance with formalities required of
8
attested wills. The Commentary further explains that the purpose of§ 207 is to avoid defeating a
9
testator's intent through technicalities and to validate handwritten testamentary instruments
10
11 where authorship and intent can be reliably ascertained. Guam's Legislature thus chose
12 substance over form-favoring enforcement where the document itself provides sufficient
13
indicia of genuineness. This approach aligns with persuasive authority emphasizing substance
14
over form in evaluating holographic wills. Courts are to assess authorship, authenticity, and
15
testamentary intent, and to favor validity where those elements are present, even if the
16
17 document's format or signature placement is unconventional. Estate of Williams, 155 Cal. App.
18
4th 197, 206 (Ct. App. 2007). The purpose of the statute is to prevent fraud, not to defeat intent
19
through technical rigidity. Id
20
Here, the undisputed evidence satisfies both the statute and its Commentary. The will is
21
entirely in the testator's handwriting, bears the testator's signature, and clearly expresses
22
23 testamentary intent. Objectors have not put forth evidence to dispute authorship or authenticity.
24
Absent evidence calling into question handwriting, signature, or intent, the Court will not
25
invalidate a holographic will.
26
Accordingly, the holographic will is valid as a matter of law.
27
28
Page 6 oflO

Decision and Order
PR0234-22
In The Matter of The Estates Of Francis L. Moylan and Yuk Lan Moylan
III. Objector Failed to Establish Undue Influence
2
Guam's statute defines undue influence narrowly-requiring proof of ( 1) abuse of
3
reposed confidence or authority to obtain an unfair advantage, (2) an unfair advantage taken of
4
weakness of mind, or (3) a grossly oppressive and unfair advantage taken of necessity or distress.
5
6 18 GCA § 85311; Hannah v. Guerrero, 2020 Guam 15, ~ 17 (Aug. 19, 2000). And while a
7
presumption may arise in the presence of a fiduciary or confidential relationship, that
8
presumption is triggered only when the relationship (and the predicate facts for the presumption)
9
are established by clear and convincing evidence-not by inference piled on inference. Id. 24-
10
II 30.
12 The Objector does not meet that standard. At most, Objector points to circumstances
13
(family dynamics, caregiving, and generalized assertions of "influence") and asks the Court to
14
infer coercion. But Hannah rejects that approach: undue influence is not established by mere
15
suspicion, and the heightened burden exists precisely to protect a person's right to dispose of
16
17 property without having their intent undone by conjecture. Here, Objector identifies no evidence
18
that any proponent used a position of confidence or authority to obtain an unfair advantage, took
19
advantage of a weakness of mind, or exploited necessity or distress.
20
The Court finds that Objector failed to establish undue influence.
21
22
IV. The Supreme Court's Guardianship Decision Undercuts Objector's Competency
23 Argument.
24
Objector's principal remaining challenge to Yuk Lan Moylan's will is that she lacke
25
testamentary capacity when she executed her March 19, 2008 will. But the record that Objecto
26
relies on to imply incapacity (the guardianship proceedings) actually points the other direction:
27
28
Page 7 oflO

Decision and Order
PR0234-22
In The Matter of The Estates Of Francis L. Moylan and Yuk Lan Moylan
the guardianship court's initial incompetency determination was later modified, and the Supreme
Court affirmed a framework that preserved Mrs. Moylan's autonomy to the greatest exten
2
3 possible.
4
In the consolidated guardianship proceedings, the trial court initially declared Mrs.
5
Moylan incompetent in an order appointing temporary guardians. In re Guardianships o
6
Moylan, 2011 Guam 16, ,i 22. But after extensive evidentiary hearings and updated expe
7
assessments, the trial court expressly revised that initial determination, stating its March 21, 2008
8
9 decision "declaring [Mrs.] Moylan incompetent is modified." Id. The modification rested o
10
intervening evidence that Mrs. Moylan's functioning had improved with treatment. The Supreme
II
Court noted that Dr. Claire Ashe's later evaluation reflected that Mrs. Moylan could "retain more
12
information than previously noted" and should be afforded more autonomy than originall
13
14 recommended. Id. The trial court also relied on the court-appointed psychologist's update
15 assessment supporting that Mrs. Moylan "appears to have the ability to exercise adequate
16
judgment for the most part," with "relatively intact" cognitive functioning. Id. From those
17
findings, the trial court implemented a limited guardianship structure that protected Mrs. Moyla
18
while preserving significant personal decision-making and autonomy. Id. at 22-25.
19
20 Those holdings matter here. Objector offers no competent evidence that creates a triabl
21
dispute that, despite these judicial findings and affirmed expert assessments, Mrs. Moylan lacke
22
the minimal capacity required to execute a will on March 19, 2008. On this record-where the
23
Supreme Court affirmed findings that Mrs. Moylan retained meaningful cognitive functionin
24
and the ability to exercise adequate judgment-Objector's competency argument does no
25
26 present a genuine issue for trial and cannot defeat summary judgment.
27
28
Page 8 oflO

Decision and Order
PR0234-22
In The Matter of The Estates Of Francis L. Moylan and Yuk Lan Moylan
V. Summary Judgment Is Appropriate.
2
Summary judgment is appropriate if the pleadings, depositions, answers to
3
interrogatories, admissions on file, together with the affidavits, if any, show that there is no
4
genuine issue as to any material fact and that the moving party is entitled to judgment as a matte
5
6 of law. Hawaiian Rock Prods. v. Ocean Haus., Inc., 2016 Guam 4, ~ 26. A genuine disput
7 exists only when there is sufficient evidence establishing a factual conflict requiring resolutio
8
by a fact-finder, and the dispute must concern a material fact-one relevant to an element of
9
claim or defense whose existence might affect the outcome. Id. The Court must view the
evidence and draw reasonable inferences in the light most favorable to the non-movant. Id. a
11
12 27.
13 Applying that standard here, Petitioners have carried their burden and Objector has no
14
identified evidence creating a genuine dispute of material fact. As discussed above, the wills ar
15
presumed duly executed under Guam law, and Objector has not produced evidence sufficient t
16
rebut that presumption. The holographic will satisfies 15 GCA § 207 as a matter of law. Objecto
17
18 has also failed to establish undue influence under the strict statutory standard, which requires
19
more than suspicion or generalized assertions.
20
And as to capacity, the guardianship record does not create a triable . dispute. The
21
Supreme Court affirmed that the guardianship court modified its earlier incompetenc
22
determination after later expert assessments showed improved functioning and that Mrs. Moyla
23
24 "appears to have the ability to exercise adequate judgment for the most part," warranting
25
limited guardianship designed to preserve autonomy. Objector offers no admissible evidence tha
26
27
28
Page 9 oflO

Decision and Order
PR0234-22
In The Matter of The Estates Of Francis L. Moylan and Yuk Lan Moylan
would allow a reasonable fact-finder to conclude that Mrs. Moylan nevertheless lacked th
minimal capacity required to execute her will on March 19, 2008.
2
3 Because Objector's opposition rests on conjecture, recharacterization of the guardianship
4
proceedings, and disagreement with the testamentary dispositions-rather than evidence creatin
5
a genuine dispute of material fact-summary judgment is appropriate.
6
CONCLUSION
7
Petitioner's Motion for Summary Judgment is therefore GRANTED.
8
9 IT IS SO ORDERED
--------
